[65.0] IN CONCLUSION 
We have attempted, 
in Campaign for North 
Africa, to provide the hobby with the most ac- 
curate and most stimulating simulation yet under- 
taken. Very little is abstracted (mostly the naval 
game), and the Players are given as much to do as 
we considered humanly feasible. CNA is a difficult 
and complex game; it is not for the novice, to be 
sure. It is our (both designer and developer) opi- 
nion that we have, in the main, succeeded in our 
endeavor. CNA was playtested for nearly two 
years an extensive list of ‘‘veteran’’ testers, most 
of whom were chosen for their insight and gaming 
acumen. Many researchers were used—including 
the inimitable Richard Garcyznski—more than on 
any other project. The amount of work put into 
the game was enormous; the amount of informa- 
tion available is almost mind-boggling. We hope 
that the amount of fun is just as great. 
DESIGNER’S NOTES 
In Brief 
A game such as CNA certainly could use — nay, 
demands — extensive background information as 
to where and why. Unfortunately, to do this sub- 
ject justice would require a veritable book. And 
you already have enough to read with this game. 
I'll simply try to cover a few subjects generally. 
The general research was done mostly by me; the 
specifics were done by a host of others. The task 
was divided into areas — Italian OB, German air, 
logistics, etc. It was done over a period of two 
years, and provided many hours of confusion and 
some laughter. (We felt that being provided with a 
complete OB for German bakery companies was a 
bit much...although the temptation to use them 
was great.) 
The scale of the game was decided by its scope and 
size — and the map configuration. The sources are 
listed in the bibliography, and suffice to say the 
game-maps are borrowed from several conficting 
sources. Often the designer had to make difficult 
decisions as to which tracks existed and which 
simply filled in the blank spaces on an Italian map. 
The tracks proved most difficult — I don’t think 
there are two maps that agree — but defining 
things such as sand-gravel, rough, and salt marsh 
was equally taxing. The map of the game is as ac- 
curate as we could get it on the scale presented. 
The greatest liberties, or shall we say, abstractions, 
are with ridges, slopes, and escarpments. Again 
the effort was, at the scale given, to present effect 
rather than strict geographical accuracy. 
The system itself was developed rather quickly. 
The first problem tackled was the theory and ap- 
plication of mobile desert warfare. Several ap- 
proaches were tried, most were unsatisfactory. 
What I wanted was a system that would enable 
units to move as far as the player wanted them to, 
but within the limits of fuel and fatigue. And I 
found the germ for such an idea in an unlikely 
place: Mick Uhl’s Gettysburg ’77. From that gamé 
came the general idea for the Continual Movement 
System. Actually, the system did not take full ef- 
fect until I came up with the idea for Cohesion 
Points — 
and, of course, 
a workable 
set of 
numbers for such. Both of these provided the 
game the one thing I wanted to prove — that 
mobile units were more powerful not because they 
had more ‘‘combat points’’ but because they were 
more mobile. 
After the movement system fell into place it took 
several weeks to come up with a combat system. 
The idea was to provide a tactical feel with an 
operational 
game. 
(The system 
for CNA _ is 
remarkably similar to Frank Chadwick’s Opera- 
tion: Crusader.) Of course, the backbone for the 
combat system is the OB work — and especially 
the values given to each unit. Lord knows where 
some of these came from, but I do know that I 
have a mountain of yellow sheets in my basement 
with a lot of mathematical computations. Tanks 
and guns were done strictly on relative strengths — 
using the Italians as a base for the lowest. The 
same with infantry. I’m sure questions will arise as 
to why some infantry is 2/2, some 1/2, some 1/1, 
etc. A lot has to do with weaponry, some with 
background and training, and some is fairly sub- 
jective. The strengths themselves are not the balan- 
cing factor, the Morale Level is. And Morale Level 
was based on performance, training and general 
esprit de corps. (The morale system was taken 
from my own Veracruz, another unusual source.) 
Combat, however, is not what this game is about. 
Logistics is the subject, and trucks are the thesis. 
And from the very beginning that is what I concen- 
trated on. Most of the design work went into mak- 
ing the logistics system work. Hundreds of com- 
putations on breakdown rates, fuel consumption, 
tonnage capacities, etc., were done using available 
statistics and charts. One of the hardest jobs was 
the Replacement Charts. It is one thing to deter- 
mine how much a man weighs, and thus a bat- 
talion; it is another to determine how much ship 
space he takes up. The same can be said for guns 
and other equipment. We had a fairly accurate pic- 
ture of Axis shipping tonnage, but there was little 
information available as to what was shipped on 
what and how. The real problem was not coming 
up with all this information, it was integrating it so 
that everything meshed. 
Luckily, the logistics 
system proved to be relatively trouble-free. 
Not so for the air system. That was hell. We were 
working with individual planes — a conceit of the 
designer, admittedly — 
and thus the combat 
system had to be tactical. However, this was an 
operational game and I had no desire to get involv- 
ed in an African version of Air War. It took a long 
evening of fiddling with a calculator to come up 
with the complicated series of computations that 
we used to arrive at our air strengths. This was 
possible only when I came up with a combat 
system. If you ask me, the whole thing is a bit 
much — but then you could say that of the entire 
game. I would have scrapped the whole shebang 
had not the playtesters enjoyed it so much. As itis, 
the air system is a game within itself, with all its 
logistical problems and planning. Oh, yes — hav- 
ing the air commander roll for refit for each plane 
individually is a good way of passing time while 
the land gameis on. 
The main question with CNA is, “Why?” The 
answer, superficially, is because that’s what the 
feedback said you wanted. But that’s a cop-out. I 
did CNA as a definitive and informationally over- 
powering simulation because I wanted to see how 
far I could take simulation gaming and still sur- 
vive. CNA is virtually unplayable — as a game. It 
is, for the most part, a source of information. Yet, 
if it is approached from the point of view of an ex- 
perience, a lesson in history, then CNA can pro- 
vide remarkable insights into a fascinating cam- 
paign. One does not “play”? CNA, one immerses 
himself in it. Some of you will, no doubt, drown; 
most will just tread water. But a few of you will 
find the swim refreshing. 
A word as to the accuracy of all this information. 
Let’s face it, I am not the definitive scholar on 
North Africa — it’s not even my main area of in- 
terest. There are bound to be some mistakes, some 
all-too-subjective assumptions, and some pure 
baloney. But all of it is based on hard research. As 
I have said elsewhere, if you have better informa- 
tion, use it. (And let me know, too....) This is a 
system for your enjoyment; it is not an ironclad 
contract between gamer and designer. One thing I 
do know, from all the research we did, this — 
CNA —is the first commerically available source 
to publish a complete battalion-level Order of Bat- 
tle for all sides at all times during Africa. Just 
think how many games you can devise from that 
alone! 
Some brief words of thanks to those involved. 
CNA took a long time (as those who ordered the 
game two years ago will no doubt attest to). Along 
the way many people gave freely (and not-so- 
freely) of their time and energy. To Redmond 
Simonsen goes the credit for coming up with the 
idea for this game — and he knows how I feel 
about that brainstorm. To our fearless leader, Jim 
Dunnigan, a devotee of the Torquemada School of 
Persuasion, goes some dubious thanks for assign- 
ing me to this project. An honest vote of thanks to 
my very good friend Howard Barasch, for spend- 
ing many hours with his voluminous notes on the 
campaign. And to my two OB researchers — 

=== PAGE 90 ===
Rae 
oe 
David Ryan and, especially, the incredible Richard 
Garzcynski, I can only say that they saved my pro- 
verbial rear-end more than once. But most of all, I 
would like to thank the game’s developer, Marty 
Goldberger. Marty stuck with this project through 
worse than I had to do. I may have designed it, but 
he had to make heads or tails of it...and he was the 
one who took all the grief when we screwed up. A 
remarkably dogged individual, Marty G. provided 
a sense of structure and parameter that was sorely 
needed. And finally, much gratitude goes to my 
wife Karen, who can now stop saying, “Africa? 
Are you still working on that damn game!” 
BIBLIOGRAPHY FOR CNA 
A Brief Note 
The books and references listed here are not an ex- 
tensive or complete list of all works consulted. 
Some works were too general to be of use, and 
while they provided interesting background you 
could not say they were used as a source. Other 
works are not listed simply because, in the long 
delay in putting the game together, we just lost 
track of the exact title (the book having been 
returned to its owner, etc.) Other works, such as 
the Commonwealth histories — including New 
Zealand, Australian, Indian, and South African 
— were definitely used but can usually be found in 
any standard work on the subject. The same can be 
said for the famous Playfair volumes, the four- 
book work on the British effort in the Mediterra- 
nean. Playfair is an incredible work, but it is flaw- 
ed. It is difficult to read, having been written by 
committee, as it were, and there is much informa- 
tion that is missing, as well as some that is wrong. 
Altogether, however, it is still the single most im- 
portant source. 
Finally, it must be noted that our two main OB 
researchers, the estimable Messrs. Garzcynski and 
Ryan, made use 
of a large number 
of unit 
histories, military studies, microfilms, and private 
letters and correspondence. 
Italian Tanks and Fighting Vehicles of 
World War 
2, by Ralph Riccio. Oxon, England, 
1977. Pique Publications. 
Regia Aeronautica, by Christopher Shores. 
Michigan, 1976. Squadron/Signal Publications.

Angelucci and Paolo Matricardi. New York, 
1978. Rand McNally & Co. 
Combat Aircraft of the World, by John Taylor. 
New York, 1969. Putnam’s Sons, Inc. 
Italian Armor in the Desert, Jon Jeffrey Kelger. 
(Source unknown; this was a photocopy of an 
article.) 
Italian Armor at El Alamein, Jon Jeffrey Kegler. 
(See previous entry.) 
A Short History of the Italian Medium Tank, 
Bruno Benvenuti. (Another photocopied maga- 
zine article.) 
The Vulnerability of the Manned Airborne 
Weapon System, Peter Bogart. International 
Defense Review, 1977. 
AFV-G2, Baron Publishing Company, 
California. (A series of magazines, from which 
several articles were consulted.) 
Uniforms, Organization, and History of the 
Afrika Korps, by Roger J. Bender and Richard 
D. Law. Bender Publishing, 1973. 
The Sidi-Rezeg Battles, 1941, by Agar-Hamilton 
and Turner, Capetown, Oxford University 
Press, 1957. 
Armour Camouflage and Markings: North 
Africa, 1940-1943. Ontario, Canada. Published 
privately, 1971. 
Panzer Battles, by Maj. Gen. von Mellenthin. 
Harman, University of Oklahoma, 1955. 
I Paracadutisti, by Nino Arena. S.T.E.M., 
Mucchi, 1972. 
Stato Maggiore Dell’Escercito Ufficio Storio 
(The Italian Official Histories). Eight volumes. 
Desert Tracks, Baron Publishing Co., 1978. 
Verbande und Truppen der deutsches Wehr- 
macht und Waffen SS in Zweiten Weltkrieg, by 
Georg Tessin. (The standard German Order of 
Battle work.) Frankfurt, date unknown.

1969.

Franzi. Tucson, Arizona, Whitehouse Press, 
1978.

(British Orders of Battle — the standard 
reference.)

Largest Unit On=Each Player’s largest unit, in size- 
equivalents, actually taking part in the combat in the hex. 
Note that only those TOE Strength Points actually partak- 
ing of combat (although not necessarily Close Assault) in 
that hex contribute toward determining size-equivalency. 
If, for example, a battalion did not contribute at least 50% 
of its maximum TOE Strength Point Limit (due to being 
understrength, engaging in a Probe, contributing to at- 
tacks in other hexes, whatever), that battalion is con- 
sidered 
a shell, and may 
therefore 
affect 
the size- 
equivalent of any brigade or division it is currently attach- 
ed to. Larger Side Is/Smaller Size Is = Either Player may 
possess the largest unit in that combat. Note that the 
numbers of the various size-equivalent units engaging in 
this Close Assault do not matter. Only the size of the 
largest unit. 5,3,2,1,0 SP’s=The largest unit’s size in

be shifted in the Larger Size Player’s favor in that Close

CAPT = If the Player’s dice sum lies within the range of numbers listed, proceed to the Prisoners Captured Results 
Table (15.7). RETREAT = If the defending Player’s dice sum lies within the range of numbers listed, his forces occu- 
pying the attacked hex(es) must retreat at least that number of hexes or suffer additional casualties. ENG=If the 
defending Player retreats after tombat this result has no effect. If the defending Player does not retreat after combat 
and the dice sum lies within the ragne of numbers listed the result is an Engaged. 
If the dice sum-does not lie within the range of numbers listed the result is In Contact. Note: The attacking forces are 
never required to perform an Involuntary Retreat.

Unit=Refers to a counter which is at full strength, a Battalion—or Company- 
Equivalent. Tank = Specifically Bn-Eq units composed of tank TOE Strength Points. 
Infantry 
= All Infantry-type units with the exception of Machinegune and Heavy 
Weapons units (whether or not they are motorized). 
Any nation’s Division- and Brigade-equivalent units may attach two non-Shell 
company-Equivalent units at no cost to the maximum attachments. Note that three 
company-eq are equal to one Bn-eq unit and that the company-equivalents must com- 
ply with any restrictions as to the type of units which may be attached to that Division

Target: Rommel 
Notes: Only by Commonwealth Long Range 
Desert Groups. See Raid on Rommel Table. 
Target: Supply dump with combat units in hex 
Notes: Roll two dice. If the dice sum is equal to 
or greater than the total Raw Close Assault 
Defense 
Points 
(ignore 
parentheses 
when 
calculating strength) of the combat units in the 
hex the desert raider survives and attacks as 
below. If the dice sum is less than the total Raw 
Defensive Close Assault Points, 
the desert 
raider is eliminated. 
Target: Supply dump with no combat units in 
hex or one in which the desert raider survived 
Die 1,2: 10% of supplies in the dump destroyed 
Die 3...5: No effect 
Die 6: Supplies unaffected. If there are combat 
units present, the desert raider is eliminated. 
Notes: If the desert raider completes the raid 
without being eliminated, the opposing Player 
must inform the raiding Player if the supply 
dump is real, 
anon-dump, ora dummy. 
Target: Trucks in convoy 
Die 1,2: 1 Truck Point eliminated. The raided 
Player determines the type of Truck Point 
eliminated, the raiding Player then determines 
the cargo carried eliminated, with Replace- 
ment Points named only if unavoidable. 
Die 3...5: No effect 
Die 6: Trucks unaffected. If any of the trucks 
in 
that 
convoy 
are 
transporting 
infantry 
Replacement Points, roll one die again. If a 5 
or 6is rolled, the desert raider is eliminated. 
Notes: If all of the trucks in the convoy are 
transporting infantry Replacement Points, the 
desert raider is eliminated without the raiding 
Player determining if the trucks are affected. 
GENERAL NOTES: All raids require the ex- 
penditure of 5 CP’s by the desert raider. No 
target in a Major City hex may be raided. *The 
raiding Player may combine attacks on an air- 
field and the planes on the ground in that hex 
into a single raid. The desert raider must ex- 
pend 10 CP’s. The results of ‘‘both’’ raids are 
determined separately with the airfield attack- 
ed first. No desert raider may perform the 
same raid in the same hex or attack the same 
truck convoy more than once per OpStage.

of the Truck Point when transporting (motorizing) the item listed. ma = Not allowed. *Applies only to Trucks in Convoy carrying Supplies. *Requires two Light

+ % x 8 for the rough + 1 each for the wadi hexside and the rough hex).

GT/OpS 
= Respectively, the Game-Turn and 
Operations Stage on which the reinforcement 
arrives 
or withdrawal must be performed. 
Reinforcements arrive in any hex(es) of Cairo 
unless 
specifically 
indicated 
otherwise. 
A 
Returning unit may be placed in Alexandria or 
Cairo at the Commonwealth. Player’s option. 
Disposition of units: All units listed as arriving 
and/or returning as part of the same parent 
formation may be placed in the same hex with 
any attachments and detachments the Com- 
monwealth Player deems desirable, subject to 
the stacking limitations. Arriving and return- 
ing units that are not assigned the. same parent 
formation must arrive in separate hexes if 
possible (e.g., you may not exceed stacking 
limitations in order to comply with this deploy- 
ment restriction). WD=The following units 
must be withdrawn. Rtn= The following units 
are returning. Unless otherwise indicated, all 
returning units arrive at the maximum number 
of TOE Strength Points permitted for that 
unit. 
Returning 
units 
arrive automatically 
upgraded, if eligible, with no reduction in the 
points available throught the Commonwealth 
Production System. A returning tank or gun, 
but not headquarters unit, may be refitted (i.e., 
is coming back with more TOE Strength Points 
that it left). The refitting may be of any eligible 
weapons system in existence at the time of the 
units withdrawal. This refitting has no effect 
on the number of replacement points available 
through 
the 
Commonwealth 
Production 
System. Parent formation and other informa- 
tion is listed within the brackets following the 
unit. ‘‘Less’’? means without. The following 
abbreviations have been used: SIZE: Div = 
Division, Bde = Brigade, Bn= Battalion, Coy 
= Company. HISTORICAL: Regt = Regi- 
ment, RTR = Royal Tank Regiment, RHA = 
Royal Horse Artillery, 
Fld 
= 
Field. NA- 
TIONALITY: 
In 
= 
Indian, 
Aus 
= 
Australian, NZ = New Zealand, SA = South 
African. If no nationality is stated, the unit is 
British. All New Zealand units, with the excep- 
tion of the two railroad engineer battalions, are 
assigned to the 2nd New Zealand Division, and 
the 
parent 
formation 
has 
been 
omitted. 
OTHER: HQ = The headquarters counter on- 
ly Arty = Artillery, AT = Anti-tank, R = 
Recon, i = 
Infantry, LAA 
= 
Light Anti- 
aircraft, HAA 
= 
Heavy Anti-aircraft. 
All 
trucks listed are 1st Line (attahced) trucks and 
may be freely divided amongst the units unless 
otherwise 
indicated. 
‘‘L’’, 
‘*M’’ and ‘‘H”’ 
represent Light, Medium and Heavy Trucks 
Points, respectively. Tpt = All withdrawn and 
eertain returning units have ‘‘Tpt’’ instead of 
“trucks’’. A withdrawn unit must be accom- 
panied 
by 
respectively, 
truck 
value 
points/motorization points (if the Logistics 
Game is abstracted) 
at least equal to the 
number indicated. Truck Value Points are 
calculated as follows: 1 Light = 2. 1 Medium 
= 1, 1 Heavy = 2. Areturning unit will either 
return with a specified number and distribu- 
tion of Trucks (i.e., listed or returns with the 
same distribution of trucks as it left (i-e., in- 
dicated by ‘‘Tpt’’ and the dates of the unit’s 
withdrawal).

Formation: The grouping of units in this sheet. 
Basic Morale: The rating for all of the units 
listed on this sheet. A number in parenthesis in- 
dicates the Morale Rating all the units ar- 
rive/deploy with. Unit Assignment: This is in- 
dicated by the position of a unit’s name in the 
“Unit”? column. A unit whose name is not in- 
dented does not possess a parent unit. A unit 
whose name is indented two spaces possesses a 
parent unit. Its parent unit is the first unit 
above it whose name is not indented. A unit 
whose name is indented four spaces possesses 
two 
parent 
units. 
The 
brigade-equivalent 
parent unit is the first unit above it whose name 
is indented two spaces. Its division-equivalent 
parent 
unit 
is the 
division 
headquarters 
counter. Certain abbreviations of unit names 
have been made. Those that are not explained 
in the Notes are as follows: NATIONALITY:

units are not an abbreviation for Rajputana

Selby Force/The Matruh Garrison 
Basic Morale: None (that of attached units). [ID Code: a 
This is a brigade-level headquarters unit that is present mostly for 
administrative purposes. It is the Mersa Matruh Garrison HQ at the 
start of the campaign for a collection of infantry battalions that will 
later become part of other, formal brigade formations. The counter 
is deployed on 1/1 but may not be voluntarily moved until it 
becomes the Selby Force or the Operations Stage in which it is at- 
tacked in a Close Assault. No units may be assigned, although up to 
six units may be attached (see the Maximum Attachments Chart) 
and these are considered garrison units for Mersa Matruh. On 
Operations Stage 2 of Game-Turn 12, the unit becomes the Selby 
Force (the only Commonwealth Battle Group). It may be moved 
freely, its maximum attachments change, and it is no longer a gar- 
rison unit. It is disbanded during the Organization Phase of 3/215 
remove the counter from play and detach all attached units. 
Historically, Selby Force consisted of the 3rd Coldstream Guards, 
one troop of the 7th Hussars, elements of the Ist Durham Light In- 
fantry, one company of the Ist South Staffordshires, 
W Company 
of the 1st Royal Northumberland Fusiliers, one company of the Ist 
Cheshire MG Battalion, the 8th Field Regiment Artillery plus 
elements of the 107th Royal Horse Artillery.

106 RHA 
22nd Guards Brigade (200th Guards Brigade) 
Basic Morale: None (that of attached units). ID Code: a 
The 22nd Guards Brigade is an infantry brigade HQ with no bat- 
talions initially assigned. However, if it is attached to an armored 
division, it possesses the attributes of a Motor Brigade Group and 
may conform to that time period’s formation organization. The 
22nd Guards Brigade is famous — or perhaps infamous — for the 
number of identification changes it underwent during the cam- 
paign. Essentially formed in February 1941 (3/21) as an infantry 
brigade HQ (with a standard complement of three infantry bat- 
talions) it underwent the following name changes: 
In January 1942, it became the 200th Guards Brigade. 
In April 1942, that became the 200th Guards Motor Brigade Group. 
In May 1942, that became the 201st Guards Motor Brigade Group. 
Units that were assigned at various times to the formation include 
the 2nd Scots Guards, the 1st Durham Light Infantry, the 3rd Col- 
dstream Guards, the Ist Buffs, the 9th Rifle Brigade, various anti- 
tank companies. 
The 22nd Guards was assigned to the 70th Infantry Division from 
February 1941 (2/21) until April 1941 (1/29). Note that there are 
two counters (22 Gds and the 200 Gds) provided for this unit. 
TOE & Weapon System(s) 
Arrives 
. 
8x 2-pounder 
8x 2-pounder : 
8 x 2-pounder 
a= The Norfolk Yeomanry 
b=The Northumberland Hussars (this is not the same unit as the 
102(NH) Anti-tank/light anti-aircraft unit). The Commonwealth 
Player may refit.only this unit'with Deacons. The refitting may be 
performed as many times during the course of a game as desired. In 
order to refit the Commonwealth Player must have the 102nd Regt 
and trained armored 6-pounder anti-tank TOE Strength Points in 
Cairo for two continuous Game-Turns, At the end of that time the 
Commonwealth Player may add the Deacons to the 102nd AT Regt 
while simultaneously converting an equal number of TOE Strength 
Points to anti-tank guns (of any type) into trained Replacement 
Points. Note that this rule does not prevent the Commonwealth 
Player from assigning Deacons to other anti-tank units. However, 
those units will only be able to add Deacons up to the difference bet- 
ween their current anti-tank TOE Strength and their TOE Strength 
Point limit. Refitting makes it possible to convert the entire unit in 
one sitting.

3/9 
Notes: 
a=This unit was attached to the 18th Indian Brigade for a short 
time, after which it saw duty for the remainder of the campaign 
with the 10th.Indian Division. 
b= This unit is a refitted version of the 11 RHA assigned to the Ist 
Armored Division. It is not a unit per se. The Commonwealth 
Player may refit an artillery regiment with up to six TOE Strength 
Points of Bishops. These self-propelled artillery TOE Strength 
Points are: not listed on the Commonwealth Production Chart as 
they. are ‘‘free.”’. The Commonwealth Player may refit any one 
Royal Horse Artillery Regiment (RHA) which is at that point in 
time assigned, although 
not necessarily attached, 
to a Com- 
~monwealth Armored Division. The refitting requires the unit to 
spend four continuous Game-Turns (one month), beginning on any 
Operations Stage, in Cairo. If the unit being refitted is the 1ith 
RHA, it may not finish refitting earlier than Operations Stage 3 of 
Game-Turn 92 (August 1942). If any other RHA unit, it may not 
finish refitting earier than Operations Stage 3 of Game-Turn 94 
(August 1942). At the end of the four Game-Turns, the unit ex- 
changes (converts) any 25-pounder TOE 
Strength Points that com- 
prised the unit before it actually began the refitting into Bishops (SP 
25-pounders). Note that if the unit lost any 25-pounder TOE 
Strength Points (for any reason) during the refitting, it would still 
receive Bishops equal to the number it began the refitting with. The 
new. counter may be used as a replacement for the old 1ith RHA 
upon completion of the refitting. (Actually, it can be used for any 
RHA that has been refit, as the refit has no effect on the ability of 
an artillery regiment to contain SPA.)

INDIAN 
This group includes all of the units colored and specified on the counter sheet as Indian. Historically, each Indian infantry 
brigade consisted of one battalion of troops of, and raised and trained in, Britain; and two battalions of troops of, and rais- 
ed and trained in the Indian sub-continent (India, Nepal, etc.). 
4th Indian Division (a) 
Basic Morale: + 1 (0)

Notes: 
a=The division was originally known simply as the New Zealand 
Division. The New Zealand Division was sent to Greece and thence 
to Crete in the spring of 1941. It returned to Egypt with the Sth NZ 
Brigade and some support units) in early June 1941 where it was 
reformed, being placed back in action in August 1941. The division 
was again withdrawn in March 1942 to Syria, returning in June 
1942. The 4th NZ Brigade was eventually withdrawn again in 
September 1942, to be refitted as an armored brigade. 
b=The 5th New Zealand Brigade (plus the 7th New Zealand Anti- 
tank Regiment and the Sth New Zealand Field Artillery Regiment) 
actually arrived in March 1941. However, they were shipped im- 
mediately to Greece. They started service in North Africa in August 
1941.

a=This unit was eventually renamed the Ist Fighting Free French 
Brigade by General de Gaulle, for various xenophobic reasons. 
Note that the brigade is one unit over its Formation Organization 
level. It may not attach any unit(s) until if conforms. In addition, 
should it lose a unit such that the brigade conforms to the formation 
organization, it is then required to obey it.

Key: CPA =Capability Point Alowance. AA = Anti-air rating. 
Barrage= Barrage 
Rating. 
Anti-armor=Anti-armor 
rating. 
Vulnerability = Vulnerability 
Rating. 
Armor 
Protection=- 
Armor Protection Rating. Close Assault Off/Def = The respec- 
tive Offensive and Defensive Close Assault Ratings. Maximum 
TOE 
= The maximum number of TOE Strength Points that the 
unit may contain. Note that this may be exceeded only due to the 
unit’s arrival/deployment TOE Strength. -=Not applicable 
(e.g., an infantry unit has no Vulnerability and may therefore 
not be harmed by anti-armor fire). ( )= May not use that rating 
if there are any units in that hex that possess Ammo and non- 
parenthesized numbers for that rating. Note that Replacement 
Points do not prevent the unit from using this Rating. *The unit 
retains this Capability Point Allowance regardless of the CPA 
of the TOE Strength Points assigned. +If the unit is comprised 
entirely of US tank TOE Strength Points, its maximum TOE 
Strength is nine points. See Commonwealth Tank and Gun 
Characteristics Chart for a list of US tank TOE Strength Points. 
4/4 
3 
+ = The unit was historically supplied with enough trucks to en- 
tirely motorize its components. Note that only commonwealth 
battalion and company-equivalent units may be motorized. If 
motorized, only the unit’s Capability Point Allowance changes 
during the period of its motorization. 
Notes: 1) Beginning with the 1/39 Game-Turn, certain Recon- 
naissance units may be upgraded (see Case 20.5). 2) Beginning 
with the 1/75 Game-Turn, infantry battalions with ID Codes of 
‘k’ thru ‘t’ inclusive may be augmented by anti-tank TOE 
Strength Points (see Case 19.9). 3) Beginning with the 1/87 
Game-Turn, infantry battalions with ID Codes of ‘n’, ‘p’, ‘r’ 
and ‘s’ may be upgraded (see Case 20.77). 4) The two units with 
ID Codes ‘h’ and ‘j’ are non-motorized (although they may be 
motorized by assigning Truck Points and the SAS may be 
motorized by a Commonwealth LDG, Case 27.88), regardless 
of all references inthe game-rules to leg infantry giving only 
CPA’s of 8 & 10.

(e.g., an infantry unit has no

this rating. *This headquarters may be eligible for augmentation

Entries are the number of that type of plane that is 
received by the Commonwealth Player during that 
month, unless a particular Game-Turn is specified, 
divided as evenly as possible amongst the four weeks. 
WD: Squadron withdrawals, the number of each type 
of squadron that must be removed from play at the 
beginning of the month indicated (except Nov II, 
1940), if any. The squadron must consist of at least 12 
planes and may be removed from anywhere on the 
game maps. Squadron removals may come from 
Malta and/or the Allied Off-Map airfields. F: Any 
squadron consisting of at least 12 planes capable of 
Combat Air Patrol. B: Any squadron consisting of at 
least 12 planes capable of performing bombing mis- 
sions. R: Any squadron of at least 12 planes capable 
of 
performing 
reconnaissance 
missions. 
*One 
squadron must consist of at least 12 Beaufighters (any 
combination of the two models). tBoth squadrons 
must each consist of at least 12 Beaufighters (any 
combination of the two models). {One squadron must 
consist of at least 9 Bomb Points (any combination of 
planes 
with 
9 or 
more 
Bomb 
Points). 
**Two 
squadrons must each consist of at least 12 planes with 
a possible Bombload Capacity of at least 
9 Bomb 
Points (any combination of planes with 9 or more 
Bomb Points).

[4.43b] AXIS LAND UNIT 
3 
It: 61 Sirte Div; Trucks: 10 L, 
Fighters & Fighter-bombers = Those planes that require a pilot in order 
to be flown on a mission. Bombers, Flying Boats, Transports and 
Reconnaissance = Those planes that do not require a pilot in order to be 
flown on a mission. Range = In hexes. TacAir= Tactical Air Combat 
Rating. A parenthesized rating indicates that the plane may never be 
flown on a mission permitting 
it to initiate 
air-to-air combat. 
Maneuver= Maneuver 
Rating. 
Bomb=Bombload 
Capacity 
and 
torpedo carrying capability, if any, in points. An ‘‘—’’ indicates that the 
plane may not carry bombs. Only planes possessing an ‘‘/T’’ may carry 
torpedos. The number to the left of the stroke is the Bombload Capaci- 
ty. The number to the right of the ‘‘T’’ is the Torpedo Capacity. 
Fuel=Fuel Consumption Rating in the number of Fuel Points the 
plane requires before it may be flown. Mission Capability = The types 
of missions the plane may be assigned. Note that certain characteristics 
are not compatible with certain missions. E.g., a plane armed with 
bombs may not be flown on a Reconnaissance Missions. A plane may 
not carry bombs and supplies at the same time. F = Offensive or Defen- 
sive Combat Air Patrol (CAP) or Strafing Missions. S = Scramble Mis-

Transport= Transport Missions. -=Does not 
possess this capability. ! = Does possess this capability, but may not be

may be flown on night missions. 0,1=The number of TOE Strength

Arrives 
= Respectively, the Game-Turn (GT) and 
Operations Stage (OpS) on which the indicated 
forces arrive. Units & Attached Trucks = The units 
and their attached (1st Line) trucks, by nationali- 
ty. The units are listed as though they were parent 
units unless specifically indicated by the designa- 
tion ‘‘HQ’’, indicating that the unit is solely the 
headquarters counter. Units listed as arriving at- 
tached must do so, unless the Axis Player is diver- 
ting a battalion-equivalent to Benghazi (see Case 
20.15). The identity of the arriving unit’s parent 
formation (if any), lack of or additional subsidiary 
units, and weapon type (where necessary) are in- 
dicated in the brackets immediately following the 
unit. The information 
within 
the brackets 
is 
organized as follows: Weapon Types of I (infan- 
try), T (tank), R (recon) and Ar (artillery); Parent 
Formations in italics or an asterisk; subsidiary unit 
changes by ‘‘Att’’ (arrives attached) or ‘‘Less’’ 
(arrives without). Trucks must arive attached to 
any unit of their nationality arriving in that Opera- 
tions Stage. Certain trucks arrive ‘‘alone’’. These 
were historically assigned to the German 15th, 
90th or 164th Divisions (as indicated). The Axis 
Player may treat these as unattached trucks (2nd- 
3rd Line). Truck types are indicated as to Light 
(L’’), Medium (‘‘M’’) or Heavy (‘‘H’’). 
Bn. It: 16 Pistoia Div [Less: 57 
Gm: 382 Panzergrenadier 
2 
1) Gm: Trucks: ag KE, 10 H ce 

=== PAGE 147 ===
: those of 
| 
many 
of thes 
__ 
destroyed 
Formation: The grouping of units in this sheet. 
Basic Morale: The rating for all of the units 
listed on this sheet. Unit Assignment: This is 
indicated by the position of a unit’t name in the 
“Unit”? column. A unit whose name is not in- 
dented does not possess a parent formation. A 
unit whose name 
is indented two spaces 
possesses a parent formation. Its parent unit is 
the first unit above it on the sheet whose name 
is not indented. A unit whose name is indented 
four spaces possesses two parent units. The 
brigade-equivalent parent unit is the first unit 
above it whose name is indented two spaces. Its 
division-equivalent parent unit is the division 
headquarters counter. Certain abbreviations 
of unit names have been made. Those that are 
not explained in the Notes are as follows: 
SIZE-DESIGNATION (actual name, not its 
size equivalent): Div = Division; Regt = Regi- 
ment; Bn = Battalion; Coy or Koy = Com- 
pany; Bty = 
Battery. (Note these are the 
English translations.) TYPE: HQE = Head- 
quarters with engineering capability; HQ = 
Headquarters without engineering capability; 
Arty = 
Artillery; AT = 
Anti-tank; AA 
=

tion for Camicie Nera (which is Italian for 
‘Blackshirt’); 
RECo 
= 
Raggruppamento 
Esplorante Companie (reconnaissance com- 
pany)’ Gaf S di C = Guardia alla Fronteria, 
Settore di Coperature (roughly translates as 
Garrison Troops), These GaF units were made 
up. Europeans living in Libya ‘‘dragooned”’ 
for the occasion. They were poor troops at 
best; CD = Coastal Defense. Counter Abbr.: 
The type as it appears on the counter’s unit 
identification 
line.IID Code: 
Indicates 
the 
unit’s basic characteristics thru the use of a let- 
ter 
key. 
The 
explanation 
of 
these 
characteristics 
is listed in the Italian Unit 
Characteristics 
Chart. 
TOE 
& 
Weapon 
System(s): Indicates the number and type of 
TOE Strength Points the unit possesses upon 
its arrival/deployment. The following are used 
for non-tank or -gun units: N = The unit 
posesses 
the maximum 
number 
of TOE 
Strength 
Points 
listed 
on 
the 
Unit 
Characteristics Chart for that ID Code. U@ = 
Understrength at ‘x’ TOE Strength Points. The 
unit arrives/deploys with that number of TOE 
Strength Points.0 @ = Overstrength at ‘x’ 
TOE Strength Points. The unit arrives/deploys 
with that number of TOE Strength Points, but 
may only absorb replacement points up to the 
maximum number permitted for that ID Code. 
Tank and gun TOE Strength Points (even if 
assigned to a non-tank or -gun unit) are always 
listed by number of each Weapons System. For 
example: A unit posessing six 65/17 Gun TOE 
Strength Points and four 75/18 Gun-Howitzer 
TOE Strength Points would be listed as: 6 
65/17 Gun & 4 75/18 Gun-Howitzer. It is not 
indicated whether the TOE Strength Points ex- 
ceed or are less than the maximum number per- 
mitted for that type of unit. Arrives: The date, 
in CNA game terms, on which the unit is plac- 
ed on the game map. D = Deployed. The unit 
begins the first Operations Stage of Game Turn 
One on the game map. #/- = The Operation 
/Stage of the Game Turn the unit arrives. ~/# 
= The Game-Turn on which the unit arrives.

b=The five artillery and one anti-aircraft units of the Tobruk 
Coastal Defense Artillery possess the following characteristics. (1) 
All of the artillery TOE Strength Points are emplaced and may 
never be moved. (2) All of the artillery TOE Strength Points may 
fire at Commonwealth warships (they are considered to be ‘‘for- 
ward’’). The numbers in parentheses for the 2nd, 4th, and 5th CD 
Artillery Groups indicate the number of TOE Strength Points of 
the emplaced guns that may barrage land targets. (3) No artillery 
TOE Strength Points may participate in assault nor may any be 
destroyed as a result of assault. (4) No artillery TOE Strength

¢=The San Giorgio was an old Italian cruiser that was partially 
sunk in Tobruk harbor. It partially blocks the harbor, but may free- 
ly fire at Commonwealth warships (it is ‘‘forward’’) and airplanes 
using its barrage and anti-air ratings respectively. It may not bar- 
rage land targets nor may it ever be attacked by land units. It may 
not be refloated, although it may be destroyed.

Kampfgruppen HQ’s (a) 
Basic Morale: That of the attached units 
ID Code: b 
Notes: 
a=Kampferuppen were ad hoc formations (brigade-level) of vir- 
tually every type and combination of units. They were formed for 
specific 
missions, 
and 
reflected 
the incredible 
German 
ad- 
ministrative/organizational flexibility. German units rarely stayed 
in division formation in Africa, and the use of Kampfgruppen was 
prevalent. The Kampfgruppen headquarters listed below are.a ran- 
dom selection of actual groups formed during the campaign: 
Gruppe Badde, Gruppe Bach (Bach), Gruppe Dedeking, Kamp- 
fgruppe Fischer, Kampfgruppe Graf (Graf), Kampfgruppe Kaiser, 
Kampfgruppe Knabe (Knabe), Kampfgruppe Kost, Gruppe Marcks 
(Marcks), Kampfgruppe Menny, Kampfgruppe 
Mitte, Kamp- 
fgruppe Pfeiffer 
(Pfeiffer), Gruppe Schwerin 
(Schwrn), 
and 
Gruppe Voss. These units may be formed and disbanded at will, 
within the restrictions listed below. If the Axis Player wishes to 
form more Kampfgruppen that he has battlegroup counters, he 
may do so.

1. A Kampfgruppe is formed simply by attaching a German unit to 
it, thereby creating the unit. 
2. The Kampfgruppe counter must be removed (disbanded) when 
detaching the final German unit (elimination through combat does 
not apply). In such a situation any remaining Italian units must be 
detached and any remaining anti-tank TOE Strength Points assign- 
ed to the headquarters become replacement points. 
3. The German Player may never attach/assign more than two 
artillery-type units to a Kampfgruppe unless it is either the Ist or 
2nd Afrika Artillery Regiment headquarters (see the German non- 
Divisional artillery units sheet). 
4. No more than a total of three Italian units may be attached to all 
of the German Kampfgruppen in play at any time.

Key: CPA =Capability Point Alowance. 
AA = Anti-air rating. 
Barrage= Barrage 
Rating. 
Anti-armor=Anti-armor 
rating. 
Vulnerability 
= Vulnerability 
Rating. 
Armor 
Protection=- 
Armor Protection Rating. Close Assault Off/Def = The respec- 
tive Offensive and Defensive Close Assault Ratings. Maximum 
TOE = The maximum number of TOE Strength Points that the 
unit may contain. Note that this may be exceeded only due to the 
unit’s arrival/deployment TOE Strength. -=Not applicable 
(e.g., an infantry unit has no Vulnerability and may therefore 
not be harmed by anti-armor fire). ( )= May not use that rating 
if there are any units in that hex that possess Ammo and non- 
parenthesized numbers for that rating. Note that Replacement 
Points do not prevent the unit from using this Rating. *= The 
unit retains this Capability Point Allowance regardless of the 
CPA of the TOE Strength Points assigned. **=If the unit is 
comprised entirely of US tank TOE Strength Points, its max- 
imum TOE Strength is nine points. See Commonwealth Tank 
and Gun Characteristics Chart (Case 4.47) for a list of US tank 
TOE Strength Points. + =The unit was historically supplied 
with enough trucks to entirely motorize its components. Note 
that only commonwealth battalion and company-equivalent 
units may be motorized. If motorized, only the unit’s Capability 
Point Allowance changes during the period of its motorization. 
Notes: 1) Beginning with the 1/39 Game-Turn, certain Recon- 
naissance units may be upgraded (see CAse 20.5). 2) Beginning 
with the 1/75 Game-Turn, infantry battalions with ID Codes of 
‘k’ thru ‘t’ inclusive may be augmented by anti-tank TOE 
Strength Points (see Case 19.9). 3) Beginning with the 1/87 
Game-Turn, infantry battalions with ID Codes of ‘n’, ‘p’, ‘r’ 
and ‘s’ may be upgraded (see Case 20.77)..4) The two units with 
ID Codes ‘h’ and ‘j’ are non-motorized (although they may be 
motorized by assigning Truck Points and the SAS may be 
motorized by a Commonwealth LDG, Case 27.88), regardless 
of all references in the game-rules to leg infantry giving only 
CPA’s of 8 & 10.

‘ww’ ID Code units are comprised of Autoblinda 40’s. Units of 
both ID Codes possess a BAR of 1 Left. 4) All units deployed/ar- 
riving with Heavy Anti-air TOE Strength Points possess 75/46 
anti-aircraft guns (see Italian Tank & Gun Characteristics Chart).

they are being transported by truck/motorization points, the unit 
then being considered to be mounted in halftracks (see Case 
14.88).
